era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt (6th Dynasty) is in its final decades. The Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin is at its territorial peak but facing mounting environmental and military pressures. The Harappan civilization remains stable. This is a transitional period: the established order is beginning to fracture, though the full consequences will not be apparent for another 50–100 years.

events.title: Old Kingdom Egypt — late 6th Dynasty administration
events.description: The 6th Dynasty pharaoh (likely Pepi II in the traditional chronology, though this is debated) continues to rule, but central authority is visibly weakening. Tomb inscriptions from this period show nomarchs conducting independent military and trade expeditions. The construction of royal pyramids continues but is increasingly modest. Administrative papyri suggest that the central government's control over taxation and labor mobilization is declining. The stage is set for the First Intermediate Period, which will follow the 6th Dynasty's end.

sources.name: Tomb inscriptions and administrative papyri from Saqqara and other sites

events.certainty_note: The 6th Dynasty's chronology and the identity of its later pharaohs are debated. This year is assigned within the broader reign context.

events.title: Akkadian Empire — Naram-Sin's reign continuation
events.description: Naram-Sin continues to rule the Akkadian Empire. His inscriptions document military campaigns and administrative activities, but the empire is under increasing strain. Paleoclimatic evidence indicates continued aridity. The empire's territorial extent is at its maximum, but the cost of maintaining control over distant regions is rising. Internal administrative texts suggest resource scarcity and logistical challenges. The empire will collapse within a few decades, likely due to a combination of environmental stress, military overextension, and internal rebellion.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative texts

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested; specific year-to-year events are not individually documented.

events.title: Harappan civilization — mature phase stability
events.description: The Harappan civilization continues its mature phase without apparent disruption. Urban centers remain fully developed and prosperous. Trade networks continue to function. Archaeological evidence shows no signs of decline or major social upheaval. The civilization will remain stable for another 400 years before beginning its gradual decline around 1900 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's stability during this period is well-attested archaeologically.
cross_references: Part of broader mature Harappan phase

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence for this year.
historiographic_note: This year is not individually documented in surviving sources. The events listed represent broader trends. The chronology of the late Old Kingdom and Akkadian Empire remains contested among scholars.
